questions.text: Who is the author of the story 'Lost Spring'?
questions.explanation: Anees Jung is an Indian author known for her focus on the plight of the poor.

questions.text: What does the title 'Lost Spring' symbolize?
questions.explanation: Spring is the 'springtime' of life; the story highlights how poverty steals childhood from millions.

questions.text: Who is Saheb-e-Alam?
questions.explanation: Saheb is a young boy from Seemapuri who picks rags for survival.

questions.text: What is the meaning of Saheb's full name, 'Saheb-e-Alam'?
questions.explanation: Ironically, his name means 'Lord of the Universe', yet he lacks even shoes.

questions.text: Where did Saheb and his family come from?
questions.explanation: They left Dhaka because storms swept away their fields and homes.

questions.text: Where is Seemapuri located?
questions.explanation: It is located on the outskirts of Delhi, yet it is worlds away from the city's development.

questions.text: What is the main occupation of the people living in Seemapuri?
questions.explanation: For the people of Seemapuri, ragpicking is their means of survival.

questions.text: For the children of Seemapuri, garbage is wrapped in ________.
questions.explanation: While it is a means of survival for elders, for children it is a mysterious hunt for treasure.

questions.text: Why does Saheb not go to school?
options: There is no school in his neighborhood
questions.explanation: He tells the author that when a school is built, he will go.

questions.text: What does Saheb find in the garbage sometimes?
questions.explanation: The hope of finding more keeps the children digging through the trash.

questions.text: At the end of the first part, where does Saheb start working?
questions.explanation: He gets a job at a tea stall but loses his freedom and happiness.

questions.text: Why is Saheb unhappy working at the tea stall?
options: He is no longer his own master
questions.explanation: The steel canister feels heavier than the plastic bag he once carried as a free boy.

questions.text: The second part of the story is set in which town?
questions.explanation: Firozabad is the center of India’s glass-blowing industry.

questions.text: Who is Mukesh in the story?
options: A child laborer in a bangle factory
questions.explanation: Mukesh belongs to a family of bangle makers in Firozabad.

questions.text: What does Mukesh want to become?
questions.explanation: He dares to dream differently from his family tradition.

questions.text: Every other family in Firozabad is engaged in making ________.
questions.explanation: Firozabad is famous for its glass bangles, supplying the whole country.

questions.text: What are the health hazards of working in glass bangle factories?
questions.explanation: The dark, dingy cells and dust from polishing glass cause blindness and respiratory issues.

questions.text: The people of Firozabad believe their occupation is a 'God-given ________'.
questions.explanation: They feel they are born into the caste of bangle makers and cannot change it.

questions.text: What stops the bangle makers from forming a cooperative?
options: Fear of the police and middlemen
questions.explanation: The 'vicious circle' of middlemen, sahukars, and the police keeps them trapped.

questions.text: The bangle makers are trapped in a vicious circle of ________.
questions.explanation: The entire socio-political system works against their upliftment.

questions.text: What does 'survival in Seemapuri' mean?
questions.explanation: Ragpicking is the only way to earn a living in Seemapuri.

questions.text: Mukesh's grandmother thinks that one can never break his ________.
questions.explanation: Her fatalistic attitude accepts poverty as their destiny.

questions.text: How is Mukesh's attitude towards his situation different from Saheb's?
questions.explanation: While Saheb accepts a job at a tea stall, Mukesh insists on becoming a mechanic.

questions.text: What does the author mean by 'garbage is gold' to them?
questions.explanation: It is their only source of income and survival.

questions.text: The people in Seemapuri live in structures of ________.
options: Mud with roofs of tin and tarpaulin
questions.explanation: The housing reflects their extreme poverty and lack of basic amenities.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT present in Seemapuri?
questions.explanation: It is a squatters' settlement without basic municipal services.

questions.text: What makes the author feel embarrassed in the first part?
options: Her hollow promise of a school
questions.explanation: She realizes that such promises are frequent in the world of the poor but never fulfilled.

questions.text: Savita is a young girl in Firozabad seen ________.
questions.explanation: She is shown as another victim of the child labor industry in Firozabad.

questions.text: What does the author suggest about the 'burden' on the children in Firozabad?
options: They carry the burden of family tradition
questions.explanation: They are burdened by a lineage they cannot escape.

questions.text: The story describes two worlds. One is of the family trapped in poverty, the other is of ________.
questions.explanation: The author contrasts the poor with those who exploit them.

questions.text: Why are the bangle makers unable to organize themselves?
questions.explanation: A combination of lack of awareness and external suppression keeps them disorganized.

questions.text: What does 'unaware of the holiness' mean regarding Savita and bangles?
options: She doesn't know they are sacred
She doesn't know they symbolize Indian woman's suhaag
questions.explanation: Bangles symbolize auspiciousness in marriage, which the young girl Savita is still oblivious to.

questions.text: Saheb's plastic bag belonged to ________, while the steel canister belonged to ________.
questions.explanation: The plastic bag represented his freedom; the canister represents his servitude.

questions.text: In Firozabad, children often lose the ________ of their eyes before they become adults.
questions.explanation: The poor lighting and glass dust lead to early vision loss.

questions.text: How many children are estimated to work in the glass furnaces of Firozabad?
questions.explanation: The author mentions that 20,000 children work illegally in these furnaces.

questions.text: What is Mukesh's house like?
options: A half-built shack in a stinking lane
questions.explanation: It highlights the squalor they live in despite their hard work.

questions.text: The author describes Saheb as 'carrying the world's ________ on his shoulders'.
questions.explanation: It metaphorically refers to the intense poverty and lack of hope.

questions.text: According to the author, what is 'daring' for Mukesh?
questions.explanation: In a community where everyone accepts their fate, dreaming of change is an act of courage.

questions.text: Does Mukesh want to fly airplanes too?
options: He is content with cars for now
He doesn't know what they are
questions.explanation: He is grounded in reality; few planes fly over Firozabad, but he sees cars everyday.

questions.text: Anees Jung's 'Lost Spring' is an excerpt from which of her works?
questions.explanation: The book title 'Stories of Stolen Childhood' directly reflects the theme of the chapter.
